title: Hand-stamped journal borders
subtitle: Printing repeating borders and header bands using rubber stamps and an ink pad
excerpt: A rubber stamp and an ink pad convert any repeated motif, a leaf, a geometric unit, a floral corner, into a consistent border that can frame a journal spread, mark a chapter heading, or divide a page into zones. This tutorial covers inking, alignment, pressure, and how to build a mitre at a corner so the pattern continues without a break.
sourceNotes: Stamping technique is standard craft practice, synthesised from Sandra McCall, Rubber Stamp Extravaganza (2000, North Light Books), chapter on borders and frames.

content.text: The craft rubber stamp is one of the few tools that produces consistent decorative results faster with practice than without it.
, keeping each impression aligned with the previous one, is the only skill, and it takes about twenty practice stamps to develop.

Press the stamp flat onto the ink pad, cover the full face evenly, then lift straight up without rocking. Inspect the face: every detail of the design should be evenly coated with ink. If any area is pale, pat it gently against the pad again. Excess ink gives blobby edges; insufficient ink gives gaps. A correctly inked stamp produces a clean first impression every time, you should not need to build up ink with multiple pats.
Position the stamp face-down on the paper and press firmly and evenly, using the heel of your hand on the back of the stamp rather than fingers. A
, pressing harder on one side, shows as uneven darkness. Press for two seconds and lift straight up. Do not slide the stamp while pressing or on the lift.

Mark a light pencil guide line along the page border where the stamps will land. Stamp the first impression, placing its outer edge on the guide line. For the next impression, align the leading edge of the new stamp with the trailing edge of the previous impression, matching the motif registration. For a border stamp designed to repeat (a length of running leaves, for example), the repeat distance is usually printed on the stamp packaging, measure this distance between each stamp placement.

At a corner, stamp the horizontal border up to the turn, then stamp the vertical border down from the corner. Where the two runs meet, you will have a small gap or an overlap. Cover the corner with a motif stamp (a corner-specific design or a small solid square) applied on top. This is how printed borders work; the corner stamp is a convention, not a workaround.
